to prevent making the previous mistake.

So uh for those who may not have been
familiar with this analysis, this is the
u probably the the siminal conclusion
from our uh uh uh business cycle. Uh we
did a deep dive study on on the US
business cycle. So I'm not a big fan of
uh academic economist theories and
goblygook. I like to look at empirical
data. If it's not observable in the
data, then it doesn't exist in my
opinion. Uh you know, I don't believe in
theories. I believe in empirics. And so
we said, okay, well, how does the
business cycle actually work? I don't
care what the books tell me what is
actually occurred in the economy. And so
we did a deep dive study to analyze
hundreds of time series to identify okay
how do they you know which ones lead
which ones lag which ones are crew
incident with the business cycle. And so
the key takeaway the one the main
takeaway from that study there's plenty
of takeaways but this is the big one is
that there is a pattern to which how the
business cycle works. It's not you know
we don't just pop into an expansion or
pop into a contraction. We don't go to
recession. We don't go to recover. You
know it's not random. there's a sequence
to how the business cycle was
historically worked. So, uh, what I'm
showing here is the median trailing
10-year delta adjusted zcore for each of
these cycles that comprise the business
cycle. There's a bunch of cycles within
the economy that comprise the business
cycle. And so, essentially what that
that sounds mathy, what it essentially
is, it's the median path that each of
these cycles takes into and through a
recession. And so, how it the business
cycles historically worked and there's
there's 12 postwar business cycles that
represent the median for each of these
cycles. So policy tends to get
restrictive. It's called 15 18 months
ahead of a recession. And corporate
profits break down 12 months ahead of a
recession. Then liquidity breaks down
three quarters ahead of a recession.
Then growth in stocks break down
simultaneously two quarters ahead of a
recession. Then the unemployment rate
breaks out right when the recession
starts. Uh so again everything's delta
adjusted. So down is bad for the
business cycle. Up is good. Uh
unemployment rate breaks out right when
the recession starts. That's when the
MBR noticed uh that's when the baggie
suit baggie suit wearing economist
noticed and they put the red bar on the
chart uh after the fact uh uh and and so
and then credit break delinquency charge
offs break out uh quarter after the
recession starts and then inflation the
most lagging indicator of the business
cycle breaks down 12 to 15 months after
a recession is already started. Jay if
you're waiting for inflation to break
down we will have gone through a
recession already.

willing to hit some kids driving down

productivity if you look at output per

a wash with regards to inflation. public
debt. If you look at federal debt held
by the public as a percentage of GDP,
the latest value is 106%. That's
applying a meaningful amount of

wages, the employment cost index, the
latest value there is 3.6%
year-over-year. That's applying a
meaningful amount of inflationary
pressure. And then the West Village
Monttok effect. Uh so uh for those who
may be new, the West Village Monttok
effect is a phenomenon that we
identified in the economy back in 2023
uh to support our Brazilian US economy.

recognized that it would support our
resilient US economy that even has for a
long period of time. And so what the
West Village Monttok effect is is
essentially uh the we have so much we
have such a high stock of of of savings
on the household sector balance sheets
as a function of the uh the historic
fiscal and monetary large we've seen
since co uh you know the the the
checkable deposits and currency as a
percent of total household assets is now
3% of total household assets. the
highest percentage we've ever seen in
the time series data going all the way
back to the 50s that's applying the most
amount of inflationary pressure and the
reason why is because we have all this
cash sloshing around in people's
checking accounts that can go be spent
into the economy at any given time. So
this is part of the reason why the
economies have been resilient and and
the and the origin of this you know the
reason I was able to notice this and and
you know without a you know academic
economist you know PhD you know the the
econ don't even realize this but the
reason I was able to notice this because
I'm someone who you know spent a lot of
time partying in West Village and you
realize you know that the people who
spend the most money in these places at
Surf Lodge and Common Ground people who
spend the most money aren't the richest
people it's the people whose mom and
dads are the richest because they can
spend a higher share of their income
into the economy week after week, month
after month. If your mom and daddy
already saved all the money that you're
going to need to retire with, you can
take 100% of your Wall Street analyst or
associate income and spend it on bottle
service. But people like me who have to,
you know, send money back home and and
actually grow a nest egg for my family,
I can't spend all my money on bottle
service because I actually have to save
money. And so the reverse of that is
true as well. is if you already have a
bunch of money in the bank, you don't
need to keep saving money. And so that's
why the savings rate has been
structurally depressed throughout this
entire business cycle. That's why
consumer spending has been relatively
resilient throughout this business cycle
because the relatively resilient
certainly relative to the elevated
policy rate and that's because we have
all this these savings on the house
sector balance sheets. Now it's
obviously not even evenly distributed.
You know, it's obviously we have a big
K-shaped economy and the top part of the
K, you know, if you look at the upper
median, the top third of consumers
account for 51% of consumer spending,
whereas the bottom third of consumers
only account for 15% of consumer
spending. So, uh, you know, it's a very
unevenly distributed K. Uh, but the
reality is that was all contributing to
uh the resiliency of the economy. But
this is contributing to a high level of
inflation. And so, the key takeaway is
that this is an economy based on all
these factors. I've been talking for 10
minutes here. all these factors. The
economy just kind of wants to have high
twos, low threes inflation, but the Fed
has this arbitrary 2% inflation target
that it got from some dead central
banker in New Zealand.
And so, of course, they're going to run
monetary policy too tight because
they're trying to get to a level of
inflation that is quite frankly asinine
in the context of everything I've been
talking about. And so uh and and the and
the only way you can really win here is
if you're the Federal Reserve, you know,
they can get lucky on the productivity
boom because uh what I did in the model
is I said, okay, on the unweighted
models or sorry, weight all the factors
evenly, the economy wants to have 2.9%
uh COBC inflation on the equilibrium
basis. But if we weight AI for 50% of
the weight and then give all the the the
remainder the the residual to all the
other factors on an equal basis. So AI
is now 50% of the model. That's how you
get to an equilibrium core PC inflation
level of 2%. So we literally have to
have a disinflationary productivity boom
for the inflation to to durably rest at
the Fed's 2% target without a recession.
So,
if that's the outcome they're betting
on, they're going to make a policy
mistake because otherwise,
this is why I've been saying, you got to
let the the the the they got to change
the inflation target. The inflation
target has to be three. If you keep it
at two, it's going to create negative
outcomes. That's part of the reason why
we have a K-shaped economy. Oh, by the
way, everybody that goes, Darius, if
they revise the inflation target to 3%,
won't that destroy the bond market? And
I'll say the exact same thing to you
that Mariner Eckley's and Thomas McCabe
said to the bond vigilantes back then,
which is, "Nope, you're going out of
business. I'm going to buy every bond in
sight that's above 2.5% yield," which is
what they did for 9 years. The Fed could
easily say, "We're going to cap the
10-year Treasury yield at 3.5% and every
bond on Earth that's trading above 3.5%
is going to be bought and sold to the
Fed." You don't think there's going to I
will start a hedge fund to buy every
treasury on earth to sell to the Fed if
they're going to cap yields at some
point, which is exactly where I think
we're headed over the long term. It may
not get there over the next few weeks or
months or the next couple of years, but
that's where we're headed by the end of
this fourth turning poly crisis. I
guarantee it. So, uh we'll wrap it up

points, positive 50 basis points.

basis points year to date. So the real
floor fed funds rate which is the floor
fed funds rate. So the nominal floor fed
funds rate which is the minimum value on
the overnight index swap curve about two
years is currently 3.12%.
The if you deflate it by the two-year
tips break even uh we're somewhere

basis points of real floor fed funds
rate. We think this 50 basis points will

funds rate itself will be minus the
basis points but we think by the end of

probability outcome by the end of next

that we continue to call for and observe
uh uh with regards to the Federal
Reserve um investors can debate what the
appropriate level of our star is until
they're blue in the face but one thing
that is not up for debate RSTAR is not a
fixed value. It must evolve as the
economy evolves. And so in this chart
here, we show RSTAR from the Lobach
Williams model. So RAR is essentially
what the real policy rate is kind of
that neither you know causes the economy
to create inflation or or cause the
economy to create unemployment kind of
from a structural standpoint. The
neutral rate is sort of our star from a
cyclical standpoint. So like what is
this current economy? What's the
appropriate level of policy for this
current economy? Fed is always setting
monetary policy with regards to the
current economy from a cyclical
standpoint. But our star is more of a
structural concept, same concept, but
just think about like just the US
economy in general like how much
population growth we have, how much
productivity growth do we have, like
what's an appropriate generally
appropriate level for the real policy
rate. And so right now it's uh our star,
the Lach Williams model says it's uh
plus 78 basis points. Um in our opinion,
that's probably too high. And the reason
why it's too high uh is if you look at
the refinancing risk in the credit
market. So this is this this uh shaded
area curve is the basis point spread
between the yield of the investment
grade credit corporate credit index and
the coupon of the index. So the coupon
is what the borrowers pay. The yield is
what the price is in the market. So when
they refinance, they're going to
refinance into a higher interest rate
regime. And so that's positive. It
peaked at over 200 basis points back in
um you know back in like till late 2022.
It's now 56 basis points but look how
long it's been positive. Like typically
it's like positive for a little bit. If
you look at the last two cycles uh it
goes positive in recession or right
before recession. We've been positive
for years. And so one I think that's uh
supportive of our resilience economy
theme. There's something going on in the
economy that's preventing us from going
into a recession with this condition.
And guess what? It's exactly what we've
been telling you. fiscal dominance. It's
the fiscal dominance regime is both
preventing us from going into recession
but also causing a structurally elevated
level of refinancing risk in the
corporate credit market. And what the
reason this is a problem is because
obviously the the the the big companies,
you know, they can they have a cheaper
cost of capital and they can uh extend
their duration on that capital much
better than smaller companies. If you're
a small to medium-sized enterprise,
you're going to have a higher cost of
capital. you're more likely to be
financing in with bank credit as opposed
to the bond market. And then you're also
more likely to have shorter duration
capital, which means you're constantly
refinancing into this higher interest
rate regime. Again, every day you go
forward, we're we've been refinancing
into a higher interest rate regime. Now,
the slope has changed, but the the key
takeaway is that it's still positive.
And so, ultimately, that just makes it
harder and harder for the businesses at
the bottom part of the K-shaped economy
to uh grow. And so, you basically it
just the fiscal dominance is
contributing to the K-shaped economy.
And so ultimately you just need monetary
policy to offset that. And if they don't
offset that then we're going to have
sluggish growth for the consumers and
businesses on the bottom part of the K
period. That's it. Uh investors can
debate what the appropriate level of
ourstar is until the cows come home. But
one thing is not up for debate. Ourstar
is not a fixed value and must evolve as
the economy evolve. Again ourstar is not
a fixed value. It peaked at 4.35 in 1973
and it's 0.78 now. So obviously it's
variable. So if you're Jay Pal or if

uh they want to separate the

oversight. Uh they want to shorten terms
and enable at real removal of Fed
governors and regional bank Fed
presidents. Uh they want to curtail the
risk of political posturing. prevent bed
officials from you know kind of cozing
up to executive officers executive
branch. They want to replace private
influence with state level
accountability. So I think that was
really important. They want to transform
the reserve banks from privately
influenced institutions to publicly
accountable bodies. The Fed needs to be
accountable. They have such a big
outcome impact on the outcomes that
consumers and businesses feel not just
in our economy but in economies across
the world. Why are they not accountable?
Why are they be separate and apart from
the political process? Why can't we have
some accountability with monetary
policymaking? This stuff matters to
everyone on earth yet we have no
accountability. Come on, it's nuts.

FOMC voting to all regional bank
presidents. So they want to kind of
create this kind of decentralized

uh, Kevin. So, keep moving here. Uh, the

bottom panel here is the OIS derived
floor of Fed funds rate out five years
minus the current Fed funds rate. So,
the market's currently pricing in five
rate cuts uh um to the floor fed funds
rate. Uh if you look at the 10ear
Treasury yield, that's the red line
there. And then the dollar is the green
line. As you can see, since liberation
day, the dollar has been creating
distance between itself and and policy
rate expectations both on the short end
of the curve and on the long end of the
curve. Prior to liberation day and and
for the previous few years, Dows was
trade was trading in lock step with
expectations on both the short and long
end of the curve, which tells you that
the short and long end of the curve were
trading in lock step too. Uh but um but
since then, you've seen this breakdown
in the dollar relative to that to those
time series. And so in our opinion, we
think that's evidence of the currency
market starting to front run eventual
regime change at the Fed uh in a way
that interest rate markets probably
cannot yet because we haven't got the
policy guidance uh from from uh from Fed
officials. Uh US dollar money markets
have already begun pricing and regime
change at the Fed. So if we look at
money markets, so this is spread here is
the yield on December 2026 futures minus
the spread on March 2026 futures. So end
of next year versus the last meeting
before Jay is done at the Fed. And as
you can see, this deeply negative spread
suggests that the markets are pricing
in. Whoever comes in after Jay and takes
over the Fed uh chairmanship will be
successful in in implementing uh you
know catalyzing a much more doubbish
monetary policy. Uh um in our opinion
and ultimately we don't think that
monetary policy pricing is doubbish
enough in the context of a positive 50
basis point real floor of Fed funds rate
that we still think is going to be a 100
basis points lower by the end of next
year. So I would position for that if I
were you.
